patterns.what_to_look_for: Long-lived secrets (API keys, signing keys, DB passwords, private keys) committed in source/config or shipped in client bundles.
source_indicators: Literal assignments: apiKey/secret/password = "AKIA..." or "sk_live_..."
-----BEGIN RSA/EC/OPENSSH PRIVATE KEY----- in tracked files
JWT_SECRET / AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY / STRIPE_SECRET_KEY in committed .env or config
High-entropy base64/hex string literals checked into git
git log/blame shows secret added and never rotated
blackbox_indicators: Secret visible in served JS bundle or .map source-map
Token/key echoed in HTTP response body or HTML comment
Firebase/GCP/AWS config object embedded in client-side JS
Backup/temp files (.env.bak, config.php~, .git/) reachable over HTTP
evidence_required: Exact file path + line and the literal secret (redact in report)
One benign authenticated call proving the key is live
Commit hash or URL where the secret is exposed
false_positive_checks: Publishable/public key by design (Stripe pk_, Firebase apiKey)?
Already rotated/revoked or scoped to throwaway sandbox?
File gitignored or not actually deployed?
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Make one benign authenticated read (e.g., GET /whoami or account info) with the key to confirm it is live, then stop.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report exposed credential with location, liveness proof, and blast radius; recommend immediate rotation and a secret manager.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Use of broken primitives (MD5, SHA1, DES, 3DES, RC4) or insecure modes/params (AES-ECB, static/zero IV, reused hardcoded salt) on sensitive data.
source_indicators: crypto.createHash('md5'|'sha1') guarding passwords/tokens
Cipher 'des'|'rc4'|'aes-128-ecb' or MODE_ECB / Cipher.getInstance("AES/ECB/...")
Static IV: iv = new byte[16] or hardcoded iv literal reused per message
Single hardcoded salt shared across all users
Legacy ciphers (RC4/3DES/export) enabled in TLS config
blackbox_indicators: TLS handshake negotiates RC4/3DES/export suites (testssl/nmap ssl-enum-ciphers)
Repeated plaintext blocks yield identical ciphertext blocks (ECB tell)
Cookie/token decodes to a 32-hex (MD5) or 40-hex (SHA1) digest
Server cert signed with SHA1 or a 1024-bit RSA key
evidence_required: Algorithm/mode pinned to a specific call site or TLS scan output
Demonstrated weakness (ECB block correlation, weak-cipher negotiation)
Confirmation the primitive protects sensitive data (passwords, sessions, PII)
false_positive_checks: MD5/SHA1 used only as a non-security checksum (ETag, cache key, dedup)?
Legacy cipher present in config but not actually negotiable?
Weak inner primitive wrapped by an outer secure layer (HMAC, TLS, GCM)?
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Run a read-only TLS scan (testssl.sh / nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers) and submit two repeated-plaintext blobs to check for ECB ciphertext correlation.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Name the weak primitive and exactly what sensitive data it protects; recommend AES-GCM/SHA-256+ and disabling legacy cipher suites.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Security-sensitive tokens (session IDs, password-reset/CSRF/API tokens) generated from non-CSPRNG sources, making them predictable or brute-forceable.
source_indicators: Math.random() / rand() / mt_rand() used to build a token
token = timestamp, userId, or md5(time()) constructions
uuid.v1() (time+MAC based) used for reset/session tokens
java.util.Random / new Random() instead of SecureRandom
Sequential/auto-increment IDs used directly as bearer tokens
blackbox_indicators: Reset/session tokens are short, sequential, or time-correlated across requests
Token base64/hex decodes to a timestamp or incrementing counter
Collecting many tokens reveals low entropy or fixed structure
Reset-link token derivable from account creation/request time
evidence_required: Sample of N tokens showing structure or measured low entropy
RNG call site in source, or statistical predictability result
Proof a token is guessable/forgeable without taking over a third-party account
false_positive_checks: Token also bound to server-side state, single-use, and short TTL?
Predictable value is just an opaque public ID, not an authenticator?
A CSPRNG actually backs it despite a misleading wrapper name?
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Request several reset/session tokens for an account you control and measure entropy/sequence; never target other users' tokens.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Show entropy analysis plus the generation source; recommend a CSPRNG (crypto.randomBytes/secrets) with >=128-bit single-use, TTL-bound tokens.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Sensitive data transmitted over cleartext HTTP or stored unencrypted (passwords, PII, tokens, cards) with no TLS, HSTS, or field-level encryption.
source_indicators: http:// endpoints for login/payment; fetch/axios to an http URL
DB columns store password/SSN/PAN in plaintext (no hash/encrypt)
Session cookie set without Secure flag; no HSTS middleware
TLS verification disabled (rejectUnauthorized:false, verify=False, InsecureSkipVerify:true)
Sensitive value written to logs or localStorage in cleartext
blackbox_indicators: Login/checkout form posts over http:// or page has mixed content
No Strict-Transport-Security header; port 80 serves content without 301 to https
Set-Cookie for session/auth missing the Secure attribute
Cleartext API endpoint responds on http:// with sensitive data
evidence_required: Captured request showing a cleartext sensitive field on the wire
Response headers proving missing TLS redirect / HSTS / Secure flag
Storage/source sample showing sensitive data persisted in plaintext
false_positive_checks: http auto-redirects to https with HSTS (preload) so no real exposure?
At-rest data encrypted at the storage layer (TDE/KMS) despite a plain-looking column?
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Curl the login/API host over http:// and inspect the redirect chain and headers (HSTS, Secure cookie); no credentials need to be submitted.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report the cleartext path with captured evidence; recommend enforced TLS+HSTS, Secure cookies, and encryption of sensitive columns.

patterns.what_to_look_for: JWT/signed-token verification weaknesses: accepting alg:none, weak/guessable HMAC secret, RS256->HS256 algorithm confusion, or signature not verified at all.
source_indicators: jwt.verify without a pinned algorithms list (accepts 'none')
jwt.decode used in place of verify (signature never checked)
HMAC secret hardcoded, short, or 'secret'/'changeme'
Public key passed as the HMAC key (RS256->HS256 confusion)
options{verify:false} / verify_signature=False (pyjwt)
blackbox_indicators: Token header alg=none or HS256; tampered payload still accepted
Stripping the signature segment still yields an authorized response
Changing a claim (role/user) and re-signing with a cracked secret is accepted
Short HMAC secret cracks against a wordlist with hashcat/jwt_tool
evidence_required: Original and forged token both accepted (200/privileged response)
Algorithm and/or recovered secret, plus the verify call site or crack result
A benign self-owned claim change accepted, proving forgeability
false_positive_checks: Server actually rejects alg:none / tampered signature on retry?
Signing key is strong and random, ruling out brute force?
JWT is only advisory, with an authoritative server-side session check behind it?
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Forge a token altering only a benign self-owned claim (or set alg:none) and confirm acceptance; do not escalate to another user or admin.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Show forged-token acceptance and root cause (unpinned alg / weak secret / decode-not-verify); recommend pinned algorithm, strong key, and full verification.

patterns.what_to_look_for: User passwords stored with fast/unsalted hashes (plain MD5/SHA1/SHA256) or reversible encryption instead of a slow salted KDF (bcrypt/scrypt/argon2).
source_indicators: password = sha256(pw) / md5(pw) at registration or login
No per-user salt, or a single global salt for all users
Reversible encryption (AES) of passwords rather than hashing
bcrypt cost/rounds < 10 or no configured work factor
Home-grown/custom hashing scheme for credentials
blackbox_indicators: Leaked/exported hashes are bare 32/40/64-hex digests with no salt
Original password returned or echoed back (reversible storage)
Account export or breach sample is crackable instantly with a wordlist
evidence_required: Hashing call site or stored-hash format proving fast/unsalted scheme
Confirmation the hashed values are user passwords (not unrelated digests)
If hashes are obtainable, demonstrate format only (no cracking of others' credentials)
false_positive_checks: A strong KDF (bcrypt/argon2) applied on top of an inner sha256 (peppered)?
Hashed value is actually a token/API-key digest, not a password?
Adequate work factor and per-user salt already in place?
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Statically confirm the hashing function and cost factor at the registration/login call site; no live credential cracking.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report the weak password-storage scheme with location; recommend argon2id or bcrypt (cost >=12) with a unique per-user salt.
